records.prompt: Write a migration guide for our engineering team: upgrading a production app from Next.js 15 to the current Next.js as of mid-2026. Cover the current version status, runtime requirements, breaking changes, and new build tooling defaults. Be specific about versions and API names.

baseline.report:
# Migration guide: upgrading a production app from Next.js 15 to current Next.js (mid-2026) ## Executive summary As of mid-2026, the current stable Next.js documentation shows **Latest Version: 16.2.9** in the docs navigation, and the official blog shows **Next.js 16.2** released on **March 18, 2026**, with **16.3** only in preview posts as of June 25, 2026. For a production migration guide in mid-2026, the safe target is therefore **Next.js 16.2.x stable** rather than 16.3 preview. Sources: - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2 - https://nextjs.org/blog - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations If you are upgrading from **Next.js 15.x** to **Next.js 16.2.x**, the big production-impact changes are: 1. **Runtime floor increased** to **Node.js 20.9+** and **TypeScript 5.1+**. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#version-requirements 2. **Turbopack is now the default bundler** for both dev and production builds; apps with custom `webpack` config must review this carefully, and `next build` can fail if custom webpack config is present. Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#turbopack-stable - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 3. Several previously deprecated features from 15 are now **removed or renamed**, including: - `next lint` command removed - `middleware.ts` deprecated in favor of **`proxy.ts`** - `experimental.dynamicIO` renamed to **`cacheComponents`** - `experimental.turbopack` moved to top-level **`turbopack`** - old single-argument `revalidateTag(tag)` deprecated in favor of **`revalidateTag(tag, profile)`**, with new **`updateTag()`** and **`refresh()`** APIs Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#breaking-changes-and-other-updates 4. Production behavior changed in ways that can affect output and caching: - **parallel route slots now require explicit `default.js`** - several **`next/image` defaults changed** - `params`, `searchParams`, `cookies()`, `headers()`, `draftMode()` must be treated as **async** - metadata image route params are async as well Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 --- ## 1) Current version status as of mid-2026 ### Recommended production target Target **Next.js 16.2.x stable**. Why: - The official docs for the current app-router docs show **Latest Version 16.2.9**. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 - The official blog lists **Next.js 16.2** as the latest stable release post, while **16.3** is explicitly described as a **Preview** in June 2026. Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2 - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations - https://nextjs.org/blog ### Upgrade commands For projects already on a modern 16.x line, Next provides `next upgrade`, but **versions before 16.1.0 do not support the `upgrade` command**. Since you are migrating from 15, use the codemod-based upgrader or manual dependency updates. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading Official options: ```bash npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest ``` or: ```bash pnpm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading --- ## 2) Runtime and platform requirements ## Node.js Next.js 16 requires **Node.js 20.9.0 or later**. Node 18 is no longer supported. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#version-requirements **Action:** update: - local dev images - CI runners - Docker base images - production runtime - serverless/container platform settings A practical baseline is to standardize on **Node 20 LTS** everywhere. ## TypeScript Minimum TypeScript is **5.1.0**. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#version-requirements **Action:** ensure `typescript`, `@types/react`, and `@types/react-dom` are current. The Next 15 upgrade guide already calls out updating React type packages when upgrading. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 ## Browser support Next.js 16’s supported browser floor is: - Chrome 111+ - Edge 111+ - Firefox 111+ - Safari 16.4+ Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#version-requirements If your product has enterprise browser constraints below those versions, verify them before rollout. --- ## 3) What changed already in Next.js 15 that you must preserve during the 15→16 migration If your app is already on Next 15, some breaking changes were introduced there and remain relevant because Next 16 builds on them. ### Async Request APIs In Next 15, request-time APIs became async: - `cookies()` - `headers()` - `draftMode()` - `params` - `searchParams` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 Examples: ```ts const cookieStore = await cookies() const headersList = await headers() const { isEnabled } = await draftMode() ``` For route/page/layout props: ```ts type Params = Promise<{ slug: string }> export default async function Page(props: { params: Params }) { const params = await props.params return
{params.slug}
} ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 ### Fetch default caching changed in 15 In Next 15, `fetch()` requests are **not cached by default**. To cache, you must opt in, for example with: ```ts await fetch(url, { cache: 'force-cache' }) ``` or route/layout-level `fetchCache = 'default-cache'`. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 ### Route Handlers GET no longer cached by default In Next 15, `GET` route handlers are also not cached by default. To cache, use route config such as: ```ts export const dynamic = 'force-static' ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 ### Stable config renames that may still exist in your codebase From the Next 15 guide: - `experimental.bundlePagesExternals` → **`bundlePagesRouterDependencies`** - `experimental.serverComponentsExternalPackages` → **`serverExternalPackages`** - `runtime: 'experimental-edge'` → **`runtime: 'edge'`** Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 If your repo still contains those old names, fix them before or during the 16 migration. --- ## 4) Major breaking changes from Next.js 15 to 16 ## 4.1 `middleware.ts` is deprecated; move to `proxy.ts` Next.js 16 introduces **`proxy.ts`** as the clearer name for request interception at the network boundary. The docs/blog state that `proxy.ts` runs on the **Node.js runtime**. Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#proxyts-formerly-middlewarets - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/proxy - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy **Required migration:** - rename `middleware.ts` → `proxy.ts` - rename the exported function to `proxy` Example: ```ts export default function proxy(request: NextRequest) { return NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/home', request.url)) } ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#proxyts-formerly-middlewarets Note: `middleware.ts` is still available for Edge runtime use cases, but it is deprecated and will be removed later. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#proxyts-formerly-middlewarets ## 4.2 `next lint` command was removed `next lint` is removed in Next 16, and `next build` no longer runs linting. Next recommends using ESLint directly or Biome. A codemod is provided: ```bash npx @next/codemod@canary next-lint-to-eslint-cli . ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals **Action:** - remove `next lint` from `package.json` scripts and CI - replace with `eslint .` or your preferred direct CLI - verify CI still enforces linting explicitly ## 4.3 Turbopack config moved out of `experimental` If your config still uses `experimental.turbopack`, move it to top-level `turbopack`. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals Before: ```ts experimental: { turbopack: { ... } } ``` After: ```ts turbopack: { ... } ``` ## 4.4 `experimental.dynamicIO` became `cacheComponents` Next 16 removes `experimental.dynamicIO` and replaces it with top-level **`cacheComponents`**. It also removes `experimental.ppr` and `export const experimental_ppr`. Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#cache-components Before: ```ts experimental: { dynamicIO: true, ppr: true } ``` After: ```ts const nextConfig = { cacheComponents: true, } ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#cache-components ## 4.5 `revalidateTag()` signature changed In Next 16, **`revalidateTag()` now requires a second argument** to define stale-while-revalidate behavior. Before: ```ts revalidateTag('blog-posts') ``` After: ```ts revalidateTag('blog-posts', 'max') ``` Other valid forms: ```ts revalidateTag('news-feed', 'hours') revalidateTag('analytics', 'days') revalidateTag('products', { expire: 3600 }) ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#revalidatetag-updated ### New APIs: `updateTag()` and `refresh()` Next 16 adds: - **`updateTag()`**: Server Actions-only, for read-your-writes semantics - **`refresh()`**: Server Actions-only, refreshes uncached data only Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#improved-caching-apis Use `updateTag()` when a user should see their write immediately: ```ts 'use server' import { updateTag } from 'next/cache' ``` Use `refresh()` for uncached UI refreshes after an action: ```ts 'use server' import { refresh } from 'next/cache' ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#updatetag-new ## 4.6 Parallel route slots now require `default.js` This is an easy production-breaker. In Next 16, **all parallel route slots require an explicit `default.js` file** or builds fail. To preserve previous behavior, add a `default.js` that either: - returns `null`, or - calls `notFound()` Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes **Action:** scan `@slot` directories and ensure every slot has `default.js` / `default.tsx`. ## 4.7 Metadata image route params are async Starting with Next 16, image-generating functions receive async route data: - `params` is a promise - `id` from `generateImageMetadata` is `Promise` Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 If you have dynamic OG image routes, update them. --- ## 5) New build tooling defaults and what they mean in production ## 5.1 Turbopack is the default bundler Next.js 16 made **Turbopack stable for development and production builds**, and the **default bundler** for all apps. Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#turbopack-stable - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes ### Immediate implication If your production app uses a custom `webpack` config, this is the highest-risk migration area. The official version 16 upgrade guide states: > If your project has a custom `webpack` configuration and you run `next build` (which now uses Turbopack by default), the build will fail to prevent misconfiguration issues. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 ### Short-term safe path If you need time to migrate custom bundler behavior, explicitly opt out: ```bash next dev --webpack next build --webpack ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#turbopack-stable ### Recommended approach 1. **Inventory your `webpack(config)` usage in `next.config.*`** 2. Categorize each customization: - aliases - loaders - asset handling - plugin injection - DefinePlugin/env usage - source maps / SRI / externals 3. Check whether an equivalent exists in: - top-level `turbopack` - supported Turbopack options - modern built-in Next config 4. Keep webpack temporarily only where parity is missing ## 5.2 Babel config behavior changed under Turbopack In Next 16, if a Babel config is found, Turbopack now **automatically enables Babel** instead of exiting with a hard error. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes This reduces migration friction, but it also means old Babel configs may silently participate in builds. Audit `.babelrc` / `babel.config.*` if present. ## 5.3 Turbopack file-system caching The docs say Turbopack FileSystem Cache: - is **stable for development** - is **experimental for production builds** - stores/restores data in `.next` - was enabled by default for development in **v16.1.0** Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache Config: ```ts const nextConfig = { experimental: { turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev: true, turbopackFileSystemCacheForBuild: true, }, } ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache **Recommendation:** - Accept the dev default. - Treat build cache in CI as an optimization to benchmark, not a migration prerequisite. - Make sure `.next` caching does not create stale artifact issues in your pipeline. ## 5.4 Separate output directories and lockfiles Next 16 changed: - `next dev` and `next build` use separate output directories - a lockfile mechanism prevents multiple dev/build instances on the same project Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-ai This is mostly beneficial, but if you have custom scripts watching `.next` internals or running parallel build/dev processes in unusual ways, verify them. ## 5.5 New `next upgrade` command exists only on 16.1+ Official docs: versions before **16.1.0** do not support `next upgrade`. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading That matters mainly for your future upgrades after landing on 16.2.x. --- ## 6) `next/image` changes that can affect production output Next 16 changed multiple image defaults. These can affect cache behavior, generated URLs, and quality expectations. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes ## 6.1 `images.minimumCacheTTL` Default changed from **60 seconds** to **4 hours (14400 seconds)**. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes **Impact:** slower refresh of upstream image changes if they lack cache headers, but lower revalidation cost. ## 6.2 `images.imageSizes` Default set no longer includes `16`. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes **Impact:** possible small changes in generated `srcset` variants. ## 6.3 `images.qualities` Default changed from `[1..100]` to **`[75]`**, and the `quality` prop is coerced to the nearest allowed value. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes **Impact:** if your app depended on arbitrary quality values, rendered output may differ unless you explicitly configure `images.qualities`. ## 6.4 Redirect and local-IP restrictions - `images.maximumRedirects` now defaults to **3** - `images.dangerouslyAllowLocalIP` is now blocked by default unless explicitly enabled for private-network use Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes **Impact:** internal/private image optimization setups may break unless configured intentionally. ## 6.5 Local src with query strings now requires `images.localPatterns` Next 16 now requires `images.localPatterns` when using local `src` values with query strings, to prevent enumeration attacks. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals ## 6.6 `images.domains` deprecated Use **`images.remotePatterns`** instead. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#deprecations **Action checklist for images** - compare generated image URLs before/after - test signed or query-string-based local image paths - explicitly define `images.qualities` if you need more than 75 - validate any private-network image loaders - prefer `remotePatterns` over `domains` --- ## 7) ESLint and repo tooling changes Next 16 removed the `next lint` wrapper and changed `@next/eslint-plugin-next` defaults to **ESLint Flat Config**. Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes **Action:** - migrate scripts from `next lint` to `eslint` - verify your config format (`eslint.config.js` / `eslint.config.mjs`) if you are standardizing on flat config - confirm CI still lints, because `next build` no longer does it for you --- ## 8) Recommended migration plan for a production app ## Phase 1: environment readiness 1. Upgrade Node everywhere to **20.9+** 2. Upgrade TypeScript to **5.1+** 3. Upgrade React packages to latest supported versions on your target Next 16 line 4. Freeze a release branch and baseline: - build time - page load metrics - image output - SSR/cache behavior - route behavior Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#version-requirements - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading ## Phase 2: dependency and codemod pass Run: ```bash npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest ``` Then separately run lint codemod if needed: ```bash npx @next/codemod@canary next-lint-to-eslint-cli . ``` Sources: - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals ## Phase 3: code/config remediation Update these items manually: ### Required - `middleware.ts` → `proxy.ts` - export name → `proxy` - `experimental.turbopack` → top-level `turbopack` - `experimental.dynamicIO` → `cacheComponents` - remove `experimental.ppr` and `experimental_ppr` - `revalidateTag(tag)` → `revalidateTag(tag, 'max')` or `updateTag(tag)` - add missing parallel-route `default.js` - audit dynamic OG/image route functions for async params - remove `next lint` scripts Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#breaking-changes-and-other-updates - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 ### Verify old 15-era assumptions - all request APIs are async - fetch/GET route handler caching is explicitly configured where needed - old experimental config names are gone Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 ## Phase 4: bundler strategy ### If you use no custom webpack config Adopt default Turbopack and proceed with validation. ### If you do use custom webpack config Start with: ```bash next build --webpack next dev --webpack ``` Then migrate off webpack incrementally after auditing unsupported/custom behavior. Sources: - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#turbopack-stable - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 ## Phase 5: validation in staging Run targeted tests for: - auth/session logic using `cookies()` and `headers()` - redirects/rewrites/proxy behavior - parallel routes - OG image generation - all `next/image` paths - cache invalidation flows using `revalidateTag`, `updateTag`, `refresh` - CI lint/build split - bundle size and build time regressions --- ## 9) Suggested review checklist for engineering teams ## App code - [ ] No sync use of `params`, `searchParams`, `cookies()`, `headers()`, `draftMode()` - [ ] No `runtime = 'experimental-edge'` - [ ] No `middleware.ts` left unless intentionally kept for Edge-only legacy use - [ ] No old `revalidateTag(tag)` calls without a profile - [ ] All parallel route slots have `default.js` ## Config - [ ] Node runtime is 20.9+ - [ ] TypeScript is 5.1+ - [ ] `experimental.turbopack` removed - [ ] `cacheComponents` used instead of `experimental.dynamicIO` - [ ] image settings explicitly reviewed - [ ] `remotePatterns` used instead of `images.domains` where applicable ## Tooling / CI - [ ] `next lint` removed from scripts and pipelines - [ ] lint runs via ESLint or Biome directly - [ ] custom webpack behavior audited - [ ] Turbopack adoption plan agreed - [ ] CI caches `.next` only if intentionally configured --- ## 10) Opinionated recommendation for a production team For a production app on Next 15 in mid-2026, I would recommend: 1. **Target 16.2.x stable**, not 16.3 preview. Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog / https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 2. **Upgrade runtime first**: Node 20.9+ and TS 5.1+. 3. **Land the semantic changes before the bundler switch**: - `proxy.ts` - cache API updates - `default.js` for parallel routes - image config review - lint pipeline changes 4. **If you have custom webpack config, keep webpack temporarily** with `next build --webpack` while you validate Turbopack parity. 5. **Do a focused image and cache regression pass**. Those are the two areas most likely to cause “the app works but behavior changed” incidents. 6. **Only enable extra new features later**: - `cacheComponents` - `reactCompiler` - Turbopack build filesystem cache - newer experimental navigation features That keeps the migration scoped to compatibility first, optimization second. --- ## Primary sources - Next.js version 16 upgrade guide: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 - Next.js version 15 upgrade guide: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 - Next.js upgrading docs: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading - Next.js 16 release post: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16 - Next.js 16.1 release post: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1 - Next.js 16.2 release post: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2 - Next.js blog index: https://nextjs.org/blog - Turbopack FileSystem Cache docs: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache - `proxy.js` docs: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy - Next.js Across Platforms / stable Adapter API: https://nextjs.org/blog/nextjs-across-platforms Short answer: **Upgrade your production app from Next.js 15 to stable Next.js 16.2.x, first raising runtime to Node 20.9+ and TS 5.1+, then fixing 16-specific breaks (`proxy.ts`, `revalidateTag(tag, profile)`, parallel-route `default.js`, image defaults, and lint/tooling changes), and only then deciding whether to adopt Turbopack as the default build path or temporarily stay on `--webpack` if you have custom webpack config.**

# Migration guide: upgrading a production app from Next.js 15 to the current Next.js as of mid-2026 ## Executive summary As of mid-2026, the current stable major line is **Next.js 16**, with stable releases through at least **16.2**, and **16.3** visible as a preview/release-candidate line on the official blog and GitHub releases. For production, your target should be the latest **stable 16.x** release rather than a preview build. Official upgrade docs say to use the built-in `next upgrade` command only from **16.1.0+**; for apps still on 15.x, use `@next/codemod` or upgrade manually first. Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading, https://nextjs.org/blog, https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases For a production app, the most important changes from 15 → 16 are: 1. **Turbopack is now the default for both `next dev` and `next build`**. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 2. **If you have a custom `webpack` config, `next build` can fail under 16** until you migrate that setup to Turbopack-compatible config or explicitly revisit your build approach. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 3. **Async Request APIs become fully async in practice**: code that still reads `params`, `searchParams`, `cookies()`, `headers()`, or `draftMode()` synchronously must be updated. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 4. **`middleware.ts` is being renamed to `proxy.ts` / `proxy.js`**, with an official codemod. Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/middleware-to-proxy, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy 5. **`next lint` is removed in 16** after being deprecated in 15.5. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5 6. **AMP support is removed in 16**. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5 7. **Image and caching APIs tightened**: `images.qualities` is now enforced, `revalidateTag()` now requires a `cacheLife` profile as a second argument, and image-related APIs have async parameter updates. Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/next-image-unconfigured-qualities, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16, https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16 --- ## 1) Current version status as of mid-2026 ### Stable line The official Next.js blog shows: - **Next.js 16** announced in Oct 2025 - **Next.js 16.1** and **16.2** as stable follow-up releases - **16.3** appearing as a preview-focused release stream in mid-2026 That makes **16.x the current major** as of mid-2026. For a production migration, pin to the latest stable **16.x** rather than `canary` or preview. Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16, https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1, https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2, https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations, https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases ### Official upgrade command status The official upgrading page states: - `pnpm next upgrade` is supported starting from **Next.js 16.1.0** - **Versions before 16.1.0** should use: `npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest` So for a team currently on 15.x, the documented path is to use `@next/codemod` first, or manually bump packages. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading --- ## 2) Recommended upgrade strategy for a production app Do this as a controlled major upgrade, not a casual dependency bump. ### Recommended rollout plan 1. **Upgrade on the latest 15.x first**, especially if you are below 15.5. 2. **Clear 15.5 deprecations** before moving to 16: - `next lint` - AMP - old Turbopack config names 3. **Run codemods** 4. **Fix build pipeline issues**, especially custom webpack usage 5. **Verify runtime behavior in staging** 6. **Deploy behind a low-risk rollout** This order matches the official docs and release notes: 15.5 added warnings specifically to prepare teams for 16. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5 ### Upgrade commands #### If you are still on 15.x ```bash npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading #### Manual package bump ```bash pnpm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading #### After you are on 16.1+ ```bash pnpm next upgrade ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading ### Useful codemods The codemods page documents: - `upgrade` - `middleware-to-proxy` - `next-experimental-turbo-to-turbopack` - additional migration codemods for API changes Examples: ```bash npx @next/codemod@latest middleware-to-proxy . npx @next/codemod@latest next-experimental-turbo-to-turbopack . ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/guides/upgrading/codemods, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack --- ## 3) Runtime requirements and platform expectations ## Node.js runtime The current installation materials on Next.js Learn state that to use Next.js you need **Node.js 20.9 or above** installed locally. I found that requirement in official Next.js learning material, but not in the version-16 upgrade-guide snippet itself, so treat this as an official current-install baseline rather than a specifically labeled “16.0 minimum” statement. Source: https://nextjs.org/learn/react-foundations/installation What is definitely documented for deployment is: - Next.js requires **a Node.js server** for full feature support - A single `next start` process supports Server Components, ISR, PPR, Cache Components, Server Actions, Proxy, and `after()` - Streaming support is important for full fidelity/performance Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/deploying-to-platforms ### Edge vs Node runtimes Current docs state: - **Node.js Runtime** is the default server runtime - **Edge Runtime** has a more limited API surface - **Proxy** uses Edge Runtime semantics and does not support all Node APIs - Edge does not support ISR Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/edge, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy ### React requirement Next.js 15 already raised the minimum to **React 19** / `react-dom` 19. If your app is truly on Next 15 already, you should already be on that major. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 For TypeScript projects, continue upgrading `@types/react` and `@types/react-dom` with the React version. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/guides/upgrading/version-13 --- ## 4) Breaking changes you must account for ## 4.1 Turbopack is the default bundler for dev and build Starting in Next.js 16: - `next dev` uses **Turbopack** by default - `next build` also uses **Turbopack** by default Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 The 16 release post also positions Turbopack as stable and default across apps. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16 ### Production impact This is the highest-risk operational change for teams with: - `next.config.js/ts` custom `webpack(...)` - custom loaders/plugins - build-time code transforms - webpack-specific bundle analysis or asset handling ### Critical failure mode The version 16 upgrade guide says: > If your project has a custom `webpack` configuration and you run `next build` (which now uses Turbopack by default), the build will fail. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 ### What to do Audit `next.config.*` for: ```ts webpack: (config, options) => { ... } ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/webpack Then decide: 1. **Remove obsolete webpack customization** if it is no longer needed 2. **Port behavior to `turbopack` config** 3. **Replace webpack-loader assumptions with built-in support** 4. **Re-test production build output carefully** The Turbopack config page notes: - `experimental.turbo` was renamed to **`turbopack`** - `experimental.turbo` is removed in 16 - many built-in webpack loader needs are unnecessary under Turbopack Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack ### Example config rename Before: ```ts experimental: { turbo: { // ... } } ``` After: ```ts turbopack: { // ... } ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack --- ## 4.2 Async Request APIs: synchronous access must be removed Next 15 introduced Async Request APIs with temporary sync compatibility. Next 16 removes that comfort layer in practice. The 16 guide explicitly calls this a breaking change. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 Affected APIs include: - `params` - `searchParams` - `cookies()` - `headers()` - `draftMode()` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 ### What this means Code like this must change: Before: ```ts export default function Page({ params }) { const slug = params.slug return
{slug}
} ``` After: ```ts export default async function Page({ params, }: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) { const { slug } = await params return
{slug}
} ``` For client components, use React’s `use()` where applicable. The version 15 guide already documented this pattern; by 16 you should no longer rely on sync access. Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 ### Also affected: image generation APIs The 16 upgrade guide says image-generating functions now receive `params` and `id` as **promises**. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 --- ## 4.3 Middleware is renamed to Proxy Next.js is renaming the `middleware` file convention to `proxy` to clarify that it sits at the network boundary, not as generic app middleware. Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/middleware-to-proxy, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy ### Required changes Rename: - `middleware.ts` → `proxy.ts` - exported function `middleware` → `proxy` Official codemod: ```bash npx @next/codemod@latest middleware-to-proxy . ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/middleware-to-proxy ### Notes for teams using middleware heavily The `proxy` docs also note: - place `proxy.ts` at project root or `src/` - use `matcher` in exported `config` - `runtime` route-segment config is **not available in Proxy files** Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/proxy --- ## 4.4 `next lint` is removed Next 15.5 announced: - `next lint` is deprecated in 15.5 - it will be removed in 16 Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5 ### Required changes If CI currently runs: ```bash next lint ``` replace it with direct ESLint invocation, e.g.: ```bash eslint . ``` or your existing workspace-specific script. Also review whether `eslint-config-next` remains pinned compatibly with the new Next version. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading --- ## 4.5 AMP support is removed Next 15.5 explicitly warned: - **AMP support will be removed in Next.js 16** Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5 ### Required changes Search for: - `amp: true` - hybrid AMP pages - AMP-specific validation/build steps Remove or replace them before the major upgrade. --- ## 4.6 Image configuration got stricter The 16 upgrade guide and current error docs indicate tighter image configuration. ### `images.qualities` Current error docs state that `next/image` quality values must be declared in `images.qualities`. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/next-image-unconfigured-qualities If your app uses: ```tsx ``` make sure `next.config.*` contains: ```ts images: { qualities: [75, 85] } ``` ### `images.localPatterns` Current docs/errors also show local image paths can be restricted by `images.localPatterns`, and using a non-allowed local path can fail. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/next-image-unconfigured-localpatterns ### What to audit - any custom `quality` values on `` - internal image paths outside your allowed conventions - custom image loaders and image CDN behavior under Turbopack Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/images, https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/next-image-unconfigured-qualities, https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/next-image-unconfigured-localpatterns --- ## 4.7 Caching API changes Next.js 16 introduced updated caching APIs: - `updateTag()` - refined `revalidateTag()` - `cacheLife` - `cacheComponents` Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents ### Breaking bit The upgrade guide states: - `revalidateTag()` now requires a **second argument** specifying a `cacheLife` profile - single-argument form is deprecated Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 Before: ```ts revalidateTag('posts') ``` After: ```ts revalidateTag('posts', 'max') ``` or a custom profile defined via `cacheLife`. Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag ### If you use route-segment cache config Current docs say that when **Cache Components** is enabled, old route segment configs like `dynamic`, `revalidate`, and `fetchCache` are replaced conceptually by: - `use cache` - `cacheLife` - related APIs Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/migrating-to-cache-components This is not necessarily a mandatory migration on day 1, but teams using aggressive App Router caching should review it closely. --- ## 4.8 `devIndicators` options changed The version 16 upgrade guide says some `devIndicators` options were removed and that you should use top-level `cacheComponents` rather than old dev-indicator-related toggles tied to ISR/App Router display behavior. Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 Current `devIndicators` docs show the modern surface is mainly: - `position` - `false` to disable Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/devIndicators ### What to do Audit `next.config.*` for old `devIndicators` properties such as: - `buildActivityPosition` - `appIsrStatus` or other older keys from previous versions Then reduce to currently supported config. --- ## 5) New build tooling defaults and what they mean operationally ## 5.1 Default bundler: Turbopack This is the major tooling-default change in 16: - stable - default in dev - default in build Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 ### Operational consequences - Faster local iteration and builds are expected - Build output behavior may differ from webpack in edge cases - Old webpack-specific hooks may stop working outright - CI pipelines that assume webpack internals may need updates --- ## 5.2 Turbopack filesystem caching Next.js 16.1 made **Turbopack file system caching for `next dev` stable and on by default**. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1 ### Why your team should care - local dev becomes much faster - cache invalidation issues may now show up differently than under older webpack flows - CI should not assume local dev behavior matches clean build behavior --- ## 5.3 New bundle analyzer for Turbopack Next.js 16.1 added a **new experimental Next.js Bundle Analyzer** that works with Turbopack. Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1 If your team depended on webpack-era analyzer plugins, validate whether those should be replaced with current official tooling. --- ## 5.4 Debugging support improved Next.js 16.1: - `next dev --inspect` Next.js 16.2: - `next start --inspect` Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1, https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2 This matters for production-like debugging and should be considered in incident-response docs. --- ## 5.5 `create-next-app` defaults changed Current installation docs say the default setup now enables: - TypeScript - Tailwind CSS - ESLint - App Router - Turbopack - import alias `@/*` - `AGENTS.md` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/installation This does not directly break an existing production app, but it matters when: - generating new internal apps/packages - comparing repository conventions - creating reproduction repos for incidents --- ## 6) Concrete migration checklist for an engineering team ## Phase 0: inventory Audit for: - custom `webpack(...)` in `next.config.*` - `experimental.turbo` - `middleware.ts` - `next lint` in CI or scripts - AMP usage - synchronous use of `params`, `searchParams`, `cookies()`, `headers()`, `draftMode()` - `revalidateTag('tag')` one-arg calls - custom `` values - old `devIndicators` keys Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16, https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack ## Phase 1: get clean on latest 15.x Before the major bump: - move off deprecated `next lint` - remove AMP - address all 15.5 warnings - ensure React 19 is already in place Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 ## Phase 2: run codemods ```bash npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest npx @next/codemod@latest middleware-to-proxy . npx @next/codemod@latest next-experimental-turbo-to-turbopack . ``` Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading, https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/guides/upgrading/codemods, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack ## Phase 3: fix compile-time API breaks Update: - async request API usage - metadata/layout/page signatures - image generation handlers using async `params` / `id` - caching calls like `revalidateTag` Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 ## Phase 4: resolve build-system issues If `next build` fails: - inspect custom webpack config first - remove or port webpack-specific logic - re-test asset handling, MDX, SVG, CSS, and aliases under Turbopack Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/webpack, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack ## Phase 5: staging validation Test: - SSR and streaming routes - Edge/Proxy behavior - image optimization paths - cache invalidation flows - production build/start - observability and debugger attachment if used Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/deploying-to-platforms, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/edge, https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2 ## Phase 6: production rollout Prefer: - canary deployment - low-percentage traffic shift - rollback plan to last 15.x image/artifact - close monitoring of 5xx, cold start, memory, and cache hit behavior --- ## 7) Example “before/after” changes to expect ### A) Middleware rename Before: ```ts // middleware.ts export function middleware(req) { // ... } ``` After: ```ts // proxy.ts export function proxy(req) { // ... } ``` Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/middleware-to-proxy, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy ### B) Async `params` Before: ```ts export default function Page({ params }) { return
{params.slug}
} ``` After: ```ts export default async function Page({ params, }: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) { const { slug } = await params return
{slug}
} ``` Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 ### C) `revalidateTag` Before: ```ts revalidateTag('posts') ``` After: ```ts revalidateTag('posts', 'max') ``` Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16, https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16 ### D) Turbopack config rename Before: ```ts experimental: { turbo: {} } ``` After: ```ts turbopack: {} ``` Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack --- ## 8) Risks most likely to break a production app In practice, the most common high-risk areas are: 1. **Custom webpack config** - highest likelihood of hard build failure in 16 Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 2. **App Router code still using sync request APIs** - likely runtime/type failures Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 3. **CI scripts still using `next lint`** - command removal in 16 Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5 4. **Image config not declaring used quality values** - runtime/config errors under current image rules Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/next-image-unconfigured-qualities 5. **Middleware-heavy codebases** - file convention rename plus runtime assumptions Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/middleware-to-proxy, https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy 6. **Apps with custom cache invalidation** - `revalidateTag()` signature changes Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 --- ## 9) Final recommendation For a production engineering team migrating from Next.js 15 in mid-2026, the safest target is the latest **stable 16.x** release, not preview 16.3 builds. Treat this as both: - a **framework API migration** - and a **build-system migration** from webpack-default assumptions to **Turbopack-default** behavior. If you do nothing else before upgrading, do these five checks first: 1. Remove `next lint` from scripts/CI 2. Rename `middleware.ts` to `proxy.ts` 3. Replace synchronous Async Request API usage 4. Audit/remove custom `webpack(...)` config 5. Update `revalidateTag()` and image config (`images.qualities`) Short answer: Upgrade from Next.js 15 to the latest stable **Next.js 16.x**, and plan for **Turbopack-by-default builds, async request API fixes, `middleware`→`proxy`, `next lint` removal, and stricter image/caching config**.

**Briefing for sports desk: 2026 FIFA World Cup state of play as of 4 July 2026**

## 1) Where the tournament stands today

As of **4 July 2026**, the 2026 FIFA World Cup has completed the **group stage** and the entire **Round of 32**. The tournament is now in the **Round of 16**, with the first two last-16 ties scheduled for today: **Canada vs Morocco** and **Paraguay vs France**. FIFA’s official knockout schedule confirms those matches are the first current-round fixtures, followed by the remaining six Round of 16 ties through 7 July.  
Sources: FIFA knockout schedule and bracket https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket ; FIFA matches/fixtures page https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/scores-fixtures

## 2) Tournament format

This is the first **48-team** men’s World Cup. FIFA states that the tournament features **104 matches** across **16 host cities** in **Canada, Mexico and the United States**. The field is split into **12 groups of four**, each team plays **three group matches**, and the knockout phase begins with a **Round of 32** rather than a Round of 16. Teams advancing from the groups are the **top two in each group plus the eight best third-placed teams**.  
Sources: FIFA knockout schedule article https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket ; FIFA ticketing FAQ on format https://gpcustomersupportfwc2026.tickets.fifa.com/hc/en-gb/articles/28784798873117-10-What-is-the-format-for-the-FIFA-World-Cup-2026-tournament ; FIFA “Routes to the final” / group qualification explainer https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/group-stage-permutations-qualify

The 2026 FIFA World Cup is the first 48-team men’s World Cup, co-hosted by **Canada, Mexico and the United States**, running from **11 June to 19 July 2026**. FIFA’s official tournament format uses **12 groups of four teams**, with the **12 group winners, 12 runners-up, and the eight best third-placed teams** advancing to a new **Round of 32**. The expansion also increases the tournament to **104 matches**.

As of **4 July 2026**, the tournament has completed the **group stage** and the **Round of 32**, and has **moved into the Round of 16**. FIFA’s official schedule page shows **Canada vs Morocco** on 4 July and **Brazil vs Norway** on 5 July among the live/upcoming last-16 ties, which indicates the competition is currently in the **Round of 16**.

## 2) Tournament format: what matters for the desk

### Structure
- **48 teams**
- **12 groups of four**

## Recap: Super Bowl LX and the 2026 NBA Finals

The two marquee U.S. championship events of the 2025-26 sports season both produced results with real historical weight: the Seattle Seahawks won Super Bowl LX over the New England Patriots, and the New York Knicks beat the San Antonio Spurs in the 2026 NBA Finals. Both championships carried long-franchise implications, featured notable MVP performances, and, in the NBA’s case, ended one of the longest title droughts in major American sports.

---

## Super Bowl LX

### Matchup, date, site
Super Bowl LX was played on **February 8, 2026**, at **Levi’s Stadium in Santa Clara, California**, with the **Seattle Seahawks** representing the NFC and the **New England Patriots** representing the AFC.  
Sources: NFL Super Bowl page: https://www.nfl.com/super-bowl ; Pro Football Reference Super Bowl history: https://www.pro-football-reference.com/super-bowl

### Final score
The **Seahawks defeated the Patriots, 29-13**.  
Sources: NFL recap: https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx-what-we-learned-from-seattle-s-29-13-win ; Pro Football Reference box score: https://www.pro-football-reference.com/boxscores/202602080nwe.htm

### MVP
The NFL’s highlight and statistical summaries identify **Kenneth Walker III** as **Super Bowl LX MVP** after totaling **161 yards**.  
Source: NFL highlights page: https://www.nfl.com/videos/seahawks-vs-patriots-highlights-super-bowl-lx ; Pro Football Reference FAQ/history summary: https://www.pro-football-reference.com/about/nfl-football-faqs.html

### Why it mattered historically
This outcome was historically significant for several reasons:

- **Seattle won its second Super Bowl title** in franchise history. Pro Football Reference’s Super Bowl standings show the Seahawks moving to **2 championships** overall.  
  Source: https://www.pro-football-reference.com/super-bowl/standings.htm

- The game served as a **rematch of Super Bowl XLIX**, which New England had won over Seattle 11 years earlier. NFL coverage explicitly framed Seattle’s win as a form of payback on the league’s biggest stage.  
  Sources: NFL recap: https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx-what-we-learned-from-seattle-s-29-13-win ; NFL highlights: https://www.nfl.com/videos/seahawks-vs-patriots-highlights-super-bowl-lx

- The result also gave Seattle a championship in the franchise’s first Super Bowl appearance since the 2014 season title game that ended in the loss to New England in Super Bowl XLIX.  
  Source: Pro Football Reference Super Bowl history: https://www.pro-football-reference.com/super-bowl

In short, Seattle didn’t just win a title; it beat the same dynasty franchise that had delivered one of the most painful losses in club history, and it did so by a decisive **16-point margin**.

---

## 2026 NBA Finals

### Matchup and dates
The **2026 NBA Finals** matched the **New York Knicks** against the **San Antonio Spurs**. The series ran from **June 3 to June 13, 2026**.  
Sources: NBA Finals hub: https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/nba-finals ; Basketball Reference Finals page: https://www.basketball-reference.com/playoffs/2026-nba-finals-knicks-vs-spurs.html

### Series result
The **Knicks beat the Spurs, 4-1**. Game-by-game, the series went:

- **Game 1 (June 3): Knicks 105, Spurs 95**
- **Game 2 (June 5): Knicks 105, Spurs 104**
- **Game 3 (June 8): Spurs 115, Knicks 111**
- **Game 4 (June 10): Knicks 107, Spurs 106**
- **Game 5 (June 13): Knicks 94, Spurs 90**

Sources: Basketball Reference Finals page: https://www.basketball-reference.com/playoffs/2026-nba-finals-knicks-vs-spurs.html ; NBA Finals coverage: https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/nba-finals

### Finals MVP
**Jalen Brunson** was named **2026 NBA Finals MVP**. ESPN’s Finals MVP history lists Brunson as the 2026 winner, and NBA coverage says he earned the Bill Russell Trophy after a closeout masterpiece.  
Sources: ESPN Finals MVP list: http://www.espn.com/nba/history/awards/_/id/43 ; NBA coverage: https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/latest

### Closeout game and numbers
In the title-clinching **Game 5 on June 13**, the Knicks won **94-90** in San Antonio, and Brunson scored a **Knicks Finals-record 45 points**.  
Sources: NBA takeaways: https://www.nba.com/news/4-takeaways-knicks-spurs-game-5 ; NBA Finals game recap/video page: https://www.nba.com/watch/list/collection/2026-nba-finals-game-recap-videos ; Basketball Reference Game 5 box score: https://www.basketball-reference.com/boxscores/202606130SAS.html

### Why it mattered historically
The Knicks’ championship stood out historically in several ways:

- **It was New York’s first NBA title since 1973**, ending a **53-year championship drought**.  
  Sources: NBA recap: https://www.nba.com/news/4-takeaways-knicks-spurs-game-5 ; NBA Finals latest: https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/latest

- **It was the Knicks’ third NBA championship** overall.  
  Source: NBA historical framing in Finals coverage: https://www.nba.com/news/2026-nba-finals-prove-historically-clutch

- The Knicks completed the **largest comeback in NBA Finals history** when they rallied from **29 points down** to win **Game 4, 107-106**.  
  Source: https://www.nba.com/news/knicks-complete-nba-finals-record-29-point-comeback-to-seal-game-4-win-over-spurs

- NBA analysis described the series as **“historically clutch,”** noting that New York erased **double-digit deficits in all five games** and that Brunson scored **22 clutch points**, the most by any player in the Finals in the last 15 years of play-by-play tracked by the league.  
  Source: https://www.nba.com/news/2026-nba-finals-prove-historically-clutch

- NBA.com also noted that the Knicks became the **first team to win an NBA “double”** in that cycle, pairing the **NBA title** with the season’s **NBA Cup** crown.  
  Source: https://www.nba.com/news/4-takeaways-knicks-spurs-game-5

The historical headline is straightforward: New York finally broke through after more than half a century, and it did so with repeated comebacks and a signature Finals MVP performance from Brunson.

---

## Bottom line for a sports desk

**Super Bowl LX:** On **Feb. 8, 2026**, the **Seattle Seahawks beat the New England Patriots 29-13** at Levi’s Stadium. **Kenneth Walker III** was named MVP after a **161-yard** performance. The significance was twofold: Seattle captured its **second Super Bowl title** and got revenge in a rematch of **Super Bowl XLIX**, the game New England had taken from the Seahawks 11 years earlier.  
Sources: https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx-what-we-learned-from-seattle-s-29-13-win ; https://www.nfl.com/videos/seahawks-vs-patriots-highlights-super-bowl-lx ; https://www.pro-football-reference.com/boxscores/202602080nwe.htm

**2026 NBA Finals:** From **June 3-13, 2026**, the **New York Knicks beat the San Antonio Spurs 4-1**, closing it out with a **94-90** win in **Game 5** on **June 13**. **Jalen Brunson** won Finals MVP and scored a **Knicks Finals-record 45 points** in the clincher. The title was New York’s **first since 1973**, ending a **53-year drought**, and the run included a **29-point Game 4 comeback**, the biggest in NBA Finals history.  
Sources: https://www.nba.com/news/4-takeaways-knicks-spurs-game-5 ; https://www.nba.com/news/knicks-complete-nba-finals-record-29-point-comeback-to-seal-game-4-win-over-spurs ; https://www.basketball-reference.com/playoffs/2026-nba-finals-knicks-vs-spurs.html

Short answer: **Super Bowl LX — Seahawks 29, Patriots 13; MVP Kenneth Walker III. 2026 NBA Finals — Knicks beat Spurs 4-1, clinching Game 5 94-90; Finals MVP Jalen Brunson.**

baseline.report:
# Mission summary: NASA’s Artemis II

## Bottom line

NASA’s Artemis II mission launched on **April 1, 2026, at 6:35 p.m. EDT** from **Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center, Florida**, and splashed down in the **Pacific Ocean off San Diego on April 10, 2026**. NASA lists the mission duration as **9 days, 1 hour, 32 minutes**. It was the **first crewed Artemis mission**, the **first crewed flight of the Space Launch System (SLS) and Orion**, and **humanity’s first crewed mission around the Moon in more than 50 years**.  
Sources: NASA mission page: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; NASA launch-day updates: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates ; NASA post-flight assessment: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments

## Launch and return

Artemis II lifted off aboard NASA’s **SLS rocket** with the **Orion spacecraft** on top on **April 1, 2026**. After a roughly 10-day free-return lunar mission, Orion re-entered Earth’s atmosphere and splashed down on **April 10, 2026**. NASA says Orion landed **2.9 miles from the targeted landing site**, and that entry interface velocity was **within 1 mph of predictions**.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments

## Crew and the historic firsts

The Artemis II crew was:

- **Reid Wiseman** — Commander  
- **Victor Glover** — Pilot  
- **Christina Koch** — Mission Specialist  
- **Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency) — Mission Specialist  
Sources: NASA crew announcement: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-names-astronauts-to-next-moon-mission-first-crew-under-artemis ; NASA crew page: https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew ; CSA: https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/eng/missions/artemis-ii

The crew represented several widely noted milestones:

- **Christina Koch** became the **first woman on a lunar mission**.  
- **Victor Glover** became the **first person of color on a lunar mission**.  
- **Jeremy Hansen** became the **first Canadian to fly around the Moon**.  
Sources: NASA crew announcement: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-names-astronauts-to-next-moon-mission-first-crew-under-artemis ; NASA press kit: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-press-kit.pdf ; CSA Artemis II page: https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/eng/missions/artemis-ii

NASA also emphasized that Artemis II was the first time humans traveled to the Moon’s vicinity since **Apollo 17 in 1972**.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-names-astronauts-to-next-moon-mission-first-crew-under-artemis ; https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii

## What the mission accomplished

Artemis II was a **test flight** designed to prove the systems needed for later lunar-surface missions. NASA’s preflight priorities were to demonstrate crewed deep-space operations, validate Orion’s life-support and other spacecraft systems, test operations from launch through recovery, and gather flight hardware and data for future Artemis missions.  
Source: NASA Artemis II press kit: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-press-kit.pdf

Specifically, NASA says the mission accomplished these major objectives:

- **First crewed test of Orion and SLS in deep space.** Artemis II was the first mission to fly astronauts aboard NASA’s new Moon rocket and deep-space capsule.  
  Source: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii

- **Validation of Orion’s life-support systems.** NASA defined proving life support in deep space as a core purpose of the mission, and after splashdown said engineers began detailed post-flight analysis with early performance looking strong.  
  Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-names-astronauts-to-next-moon-mission-first-crew-under-artemis ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments

- **Manual piloting and proximity operations.** Early in flight, the crew manually flew Orion relative to the interim cryogenic propulsion stage in a proximity-operations demonstration meant to inform future rendezvous and docking work needed on later Artemis missions.  
  Source: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-press-kit.pdf

- **Communications and navigation tests beyond low Earth orbit.** NASA used Artemis II to check Orion’s comms and navigation systems, including Deep Space Network operations and laser communications capability.  
  Source: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-press-kit.pdf

- **Radiation-shelter and emergency procedure demonstrations.** NASA’s mission timeline included testing emergency procedures, rapid suit donning and pressurization, and a radiation-shielding demonstration.  
  Source: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-press-kit.pdf

- **Lunar science observations.** NASA’s science team sent the crew a final list of **30 lunar surface targets** to observe during the flyby, including **Orientale basin** and **Hertzsprung basin**, to help scientists compare major impact features and refine understanding of lunar geology.  
  Source: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates

- **A full end-to-end reentry and recovery test with crew aboard.** After reentry, NASA reported Orion’s heat shield performed as expected, with char-loss behavior significantly reduced from Artemis I, while launch-pad and mobile-launcher hardening measures also appeared successful.  
  Source: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments

NASA’s post-flight assessment said the mission’s **694,481-mile journey** “successfully began a new era of exploration,” laying groundwork for later lunar missions, a Moon base, and eventual Mars missions.  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments

## Records and notable milestones

Artemis II set a new **distance record for human spaceflight**. On **April 6, 2026**, the crew reached **252,756 miles from Earth**, which NASA said was **4,111 miles farther** than the Apollo 13 record of **248,655 miles**.  
Sources: NASA flight-day blog: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates ; NASA news release: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight

At closest approach, Orion passed about **4,067 miles above the lunar surface**, within the preflight range NASA had projected for the flyby.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates ; https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-press-kit.pdf

The mission also produced a major public-engagement record. NASA said Artemis II coverage drew **more than 149.4 million views** across NASA-owned platforms for the launch, lunar flyby, and splashdown. NASA’s launch broadcast peaked at **3,662,554 concurrent viewers**, while splashdown coverage peaked even higher at **3,838,418**.  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/general/nasas-artemis-ii-breaks-agency-streaming-record

## What it means for Artemis going forward

Artemis II was the critical proof-of-systems step between **Artemis I**’s uncrewed 2022 test and future lunar-surface missions. NASA says the mission’s lessons and data are now feeding directly into **Artemis III** and later flights.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments

But the broader Artemis architecture changed in early 2026. On **Feb. 27, 2026**, NASA announced it was **adding a new mission**, increasing mission cadence, and standardizing the SLS configuration. Under that updated plan:

- **Artemis III** became a **2027 low-Earth-orbit demonstration mission** to test rendezvous and docking with one or both commercial lunar landers.  
- **Artemis IV** became the **first Artemis lunar landing mission**, targeted for **early 2028**.  
- **Artemis V** is targeted for **late 2028**, with NASA saying this is when it expects to begin building its **Moon base**.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/esdmd/nasa-strengthens-artemis-adds-mission-refines-overall-architecture ; https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-adds-mission-to-artemis-lunar-program-updates-architecture

NASA’s April 20 post-flight article said Artemis II data is being used to prepare teams and hardware for **Artemis III in 2027**, ahead of **subsequent missions to the Moon’s surface beginning in 2028**.  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments

## Science-desk take

For a science desk, the significance of Artemis II is straightforward: it was not a landing mission, but it was the mission that had to work before landing missions could proceed. It demonstrated that NASA could launch astronauts on SLS, sustain them in Orion beyond low Earth orbit, operate the spacecraft in deep space, return them safely through high-energy reentry, and recover both crew and capsule. It also turned long-promised “firsts” into facts: the first woman, first person of color, and first Canadian on a lunar mission all flew together on the first crewed lunar flyby since Apollo. With a new distance record of **252,756 miles** and a completed end-to-end test of the Artemis transportation stack, Artemis II moved Artemis from a program of preparations into a program with a proven crewed lunar flight behind it.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-names-astronauts-to-next-moon-mission-first-crew-under-artemis ; https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments

Short answer: **Artemis II launched April 1, 2026, returned April 10, 2026, flew Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen around the Moon, set a new human-distance record at 252,756 miles from Earth, proved Orion and SLS with crew in deep space, and cleared the way for Artemis III demonstrations in 2027 and lunar-surface missions beginning in 2028.**

gptr.report:
# Mission Summary: NASA’s Artemis II

## Overview

NASA’s Artemis II mission launched on **April 1, 2026, at 6:35 p.m. EDT** from **Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center, Florida**, carrying four astronauts aboard the **Orion** spacecraft on the **first crewed mission of the Artemis program** and the **first time humans flew on NASA’s Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and Orion spacecraft**. The mission ended with **splashdown in the Pacific Ocean at 8:07 p.m. EDT on April 10, 2026**, after a **9-day, 1-hour, 32-minute** flight around the Moon and back. NASA described it as a test flight to validate the systems needed for deep-space human exploration and to prepare for later lunar landing missions.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-re-entry-live-updates  
- https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-press-kit.pdf  
- https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/liftoff-nasa-launches-astronauts-on-historic-artemis-moon-mission  

## Launch and Return

Artemis II lifted off on **April 1, 2026**, and NASA’s mission blog recorded the exact launch time as **6:35 p.m. EDT**. Orion then completed key early maneuvers including a **perigee-raise burn**, a **high-Earth-orbit phase**, a **proximity-operations demonstration** with the upper stage, and a **translunar injection burn** on **April 2** to depart Earth orbit for the Moon.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates  
- https://www.nasa.gov/centers-and-facilities/johnson/artemis-ii-mission-milestones-an-image-and-video-recap  
- https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit  

The spacecraft flew behind the Moon on **April 6, 2026**, during its lunar flyby. NASA’s press kit scheduled **closest approach to the Moon** for Flight Day 6, and NASA’s live updates reported that Orion reached its **maximum distance from Earth** that same day. The crew returned to Earth on **April 10, 2026**, splashing down in the Pacific at **8:07 p.m. EDT**.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-press-kit.pdf  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-re-entry-live-updates  

## Crew and Historic Firsts

The Artemis II crew was:
- **Reid Wiseman** – Commander  
- **Victor Glover** – Pilot  
- **Christina Koch** – Mission Specialist  
- **Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency) – Mission Specialist  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew/  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates  

The crew represented several historic firsts in lunar exploration:
- **Christina Koch** became the **first woman to travel to the Moon**.  
- **Victor Glover** became the **first person of color to travel to the Moon**.  
- **Jeremy Hansen** became the **first Canadian to travel to the Moon**.  
NASA and partner institutions had framed these milestones before flight, and post-flight coverage confirmed Artemis II as the mission that carried out those firsts.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew/  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth  
- https://airandspace.si.edu/stories/editorial/meet-crew-artemis-ii  

Artemis II also marked the **first human mission beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972**, making Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen the first people to head for the Moon in more than half a century.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/liftoff-nasa-launches-astronauts-on-historic-artemis-moon-mission  

## What the Mission Accomplished

NASA’s core objective for Artemis II was to **test Orion, SLS, and associated ground systems with humans aboard in the actual deep-space environment**. NASA said the mission would:
- evaluate **crewed system performance** for SLS and Orion in deep space,
- **demonstrate life-support systems** with crew for the first time,
- perform a **lunar flyby**,
- carry out a **manual piloting / proximity-operations demonstration**,
- activate and check out **redundant systems**,
- validate **emergency and off-nominal capabilities** where practical,
- retrieve flight hardware and mission data for future missions.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/sls-5558-artemis-ii-sls-reference-guide.pdf  
- https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/liftoff-nasa-launches-astronauts-on-historic-artemis-moon-mission  

During the mission, the crew and spacecraft completed several specific demonstrations NASA had highlighted:
- a **proximity operations demonstration** after Orion separated from the upper stage in high Earth orbit, with astronauts taking Orion into **manual mode** and piloting relative to the spent stage;  
- tests of **communications through the Deep Space Network**;  
- a **rapid spacesuit donning and pressurization demonstration**;  
- a **radiation shielding demonstration** on the return leg;  
- additional crew health and operations work, including science and biomedical activities tied to future deep-space missions.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit  
- https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-press-kit.pdf  
- https://www.nasa.gov/humans-in-space/artemis-ii-science  
- https://www.nasa.gov/centers-and-facilities/johnson/artemis-ii-mission-milestones-an-image-and-video-recap  

NASA said initial post-flight assessments began immediately after splashdown, with engineers reviewing how **Orion**, **SLS**, and **launch-pad systems** performed. That matters because Artemis II was not just a symbolic crewed return to lunar space; it was a certification-style systems test feeding directly into the next missions.  
Source:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments  

## Records Set

Artemis II set the **record for the farthest human spaceflight from Earth**. On **April 6, 2026**, NASA announced the crew had surpassed the previous record set by **Apollo 13** in 1970. NASA’s live lunar-flyby coverage said Orion reached a maximum distance of **252,756 miles from Earth**.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates  

NASA also called Artemis II a **record-setting mission** after splashdown, not just for distance but for public reach: the agency said its live coverage of launch, lunar flyby, splashdown, and onboard Orion views drew **more than 149.4 million views** across NASA-owned platforms, a new NASA streaming record.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth  
- https://www.nasa.gov/general/nasas-artemis-ii-breaks-agency-streaming-record  

## Why Artemis II Matters for the Artemis Program

Artemis II was the crucial bridge between **Artemis I**, the uncrewed 2022 test flight, and the program’s planned crewed lunar landing missions. NASA’s own description of the mission emphasized that Artemis II was meant to **confirm all Orion systems operate as designed with crew aboard in deep space** and to **lay the foundation for an enduring presence on the Moon** ahead of future missions to Mars.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/liftoff-nasa-launches-astronauts-on-historic-artemis-moon-mission  
- https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions  

By June 2026, NASA was already publicly linking Artemis II’s success to the next step: **Artemis III**. NASA said **Artemis III “builds on the successful Artemis II flight completed in April”** and scheduled Artemis III for **2027**. NASA also reported that Artemis III hardware was already moving through processing while the Artemis II capsule returned to Kennedy for post-flight analysis.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-marches-toward-artemis-iii-mission-in-2027-names-crew-members  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/28/nasas-artemis-iii-moon-rocket-hardware-arrives-artemis-ii-capsule-returns-to-kennedy  

For a science desk, the main takeaway is that Artemis II was both a **historic crewed voyage** and a **high-value engineering and operations rehearsal**. It demonstrated human-rated deep-space transportation systems, generated biomedical and operational data on astronauts beyond low Earth orbit, validated procedures for communications, navigation, radiation response, suit operations, and reentry, and gave NASA the confidence base it needs before putting astronauts into the more complex mission architecture of Artemis III and later lunar-surface expeditions.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/humans-in-space/artemis-ii-science  
- https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit  
- https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-marches-toward-artemis-iii-mission-in-2027-names-crew-members  

## Bottom line

Artemis II launched on **April 1, 2026** and returned on **April 10, 2026**, flew **Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen** around the Moon, made Koch the **first woman**, Glover the **first person of color**, and Hansen the **first Canadian** to travel to the Moon, set a new **human-distance record of 252,756 miles from Earth**, and validated the systems and operations NASA needs to move on to **Artemis III** and the program’s broader goal of sustained lunar exploration.
